name: The Preservers
description: Keep the mission, the culture, and the bloodlines exactly as the founders intended. Every deviation is a small extinction.

dilemmas.title: The Living Archive
dilemmas.description: The archive decks need power the farms want. The archivists call the records the ship's soul; the agronomists call them dead weight.

success.log: The archive kept its power allocation. Tradition holds.

failure.log: The archive was protected, and the harvest paid for it.

success.log: The farms flourished. Something older dimmed.

failure.log: The diversion sparked protests in the archive decks.

dilemmas.title: The Drift Dialect
dilemmas.description: The children speak a ship-tongue the founders would not follow. The linguists want it recorded and taught; the elders want it corrected.

success.log: The old grammar was drilled back into the decks. The founders would understand every word.

failure.log: The correction landed as a rebuke. The children obey, and resent.

success.log: The ship-tongue entered the archive as a living thing. The founders' words are now one dialect among two.

failure.log: The archive swelled with a tongue no elder will read. The soul of the record blurred.

dilemmas.title: The Founder's Chair
dilemmas.description: The founder's seat has sat empty on the council for six generations, a place kept for a woman long dead. A movement wants the living leader to finally sit in it.

success.log: The chair stays empty, and full. The founder still presides over every vote.

failure.log: The empty chair began to look like a rebuke to the living. Some stopped attending.

success.log: The leader took the founder's chair. The council feels present-tense for the first time in memory.

failure.log: The living leader sat in the founder's chair, and something sacred became merely furniture.

dilemmas.title: The Calendar Question
dilemmas.description: The ship keeps two clocks: the founders' Earth-dated calendar and the drift of ship-years. The council must pick one to be official.

success.log: The ship keeps founders' time. Every date still points home.

failure.log: Founders' time held, but the young count the mismatch and shrug.

success.log: The ship keeps its own time now. The calendar finally matches the sky outside.

failure.log: Ship-time won, and a thousand founder anniversaries quietly went uncelebrated.

dilemmas.title: The Bloodline Registry
dilemmas.description: The registrars propose formal lineage records and matched pairings to keep the founding bloodlines legible for generations to come.

success.log: The bloodlines were charted and shepherded. The founders' names march on in order.

failure.log: The registry held, but the matched pairings bred as much resentment as heirs.

success.log: The registry was shelved. The bloodlines tangle now, happily and untraceably.

failure.log: Without the registry, three founding names quietly vanished into the crowd.

dilemmas.title: The Relic Engine
dilemmas.description: A founding-era drive component still turns in the reactor hall, venerated and inefficient. Engineering has a replacement that would insult it.

success.log: The relic engine turns on, worshipped and wasteful. The founders' hands are still on the ship.

failure.log: The relic kept turning and kept drinking power. Devotion has an energy bill.

options.label: Retire it for the new drive

success.log: The relic was retired to a display case. The new drive is quieter and colder and better.

failure.log: The relic came out and something in the ship's chest went quiet. Efficiency has a cost too.

dilemmas.title: The Sealed Deck
dilemmas.description: The founders welded shut one deck and left no reason in the record. Six generations later a leak forces the question: cut it open, or let the seal — and its silence — hold.

success.log: The sealed deck stayed sealed. Whatever the founders hid, the ship still trusts them to have had a reason.

failure.log: The seal held, and the leak was rerouted at cost. The deck keeps its secret and its silence.

success.log: The sealed deck was opened. It held only old machinery and older fear. The founders were not infallible after all.

failure.log: The deck was opened, and what the founders sealed away is a matter of argument now, not faith.

dilemmas.title: The Apostate
dilemmas.description: A respected elder has stood before the council and called the mission a corpse the ship is still dressing each morning. The decks are listening.

success.log: The apostate was answered, not silenced. The mission survived the argument stronger for having had it.

failure.log: The debate went badly. The apostate had better questions than the mission had answers.

success.log: The apostate was stripped of their council seat. The mission's letter was upheld; its spirit is quieter.

failure.log: Silencing the apostate made a martyr of them. The decks kept the questions the elder was no longer allowed to ask.

name: The Adaptors
description: The void does not negotiate. Change the crew — genetically, cybernetically, biologically — until the mission fits.

dilemmas.title: The Gene Clinic
dilemmas.description: A radiation-resistance germline edit is ready for the next birth cohort. It has never been tried on a full generation.

success.log: The edited cohort thrived. The ship is becoming something new.

failure.log: Complications in the edited cohort haunted the med decks for years.

success.log: The trials continued cautiously. No breakthroughs, no funerals.

failure.log: Caution calcified into inertia. The clinic lost its best minds.

dilemmas.title: The Long Dark
dilemmas.description: A lightless twenty-year leg lies ahead. Engineering has a hibernation edit that would let most of the ship sleep through it — untested at scale.

success.log: The ship slept through the dark on a fraction of its rations. It woke changed, and fed.

failure.log: Not everyone woke right. The med decks do not discuss the ones who woke wrong.

success.log: The ship stayed awake through the dark, together, hungry, and whole.

failure.log: Twenty years of dark with open eyes. The counselors earned their keep.

dilemmas.title: The Mirror Test
dilemmas.description: The newest cohort, three edits deep, looked at the founders' portraits and felt nothing. A chaplain asks whether they are still the same crew, or a new one wearing the ship.

success.log: The ship stopped mourning the crew it used to be. The mirror shows something that survives.

failure.log: Told they were something new, the cohort grieved a self they never had.

success.log: The portraits went back up in every ward. The cohort learned whose faces to miss.

failure.log: The anchoring rang hollow. You cannot miss a face you were built to forget.

dilemmas.title: The Neural Lace
dilemmas.description: A brain-machine lace would let the crew think with the ship. The first volunteers are already queuing; the ethicists are already resigning.

success.log: The laced crew move like one mind at the consoles. The ship has never run so smooth.

failure.log: Some laces took wrong. The ward speaks softly of the ones who can't stop hearing the ship.

success.log: The lace was banned. The crew keep their own thoughts, and their own inefficiencies.

failure.log: The ban held, and the best minds left for ships less squeamish. The void does not wait.

dilemmas.title: The Ageless Trial
dilemmas.description: A longevity edit could stop the crew aging entirely. A dynasty that never dies is a dynasty that never inherits.

success.log: The edited elders stopped aging. The ship is older and younger than it has ever been.

failure.log: The ageless walk the decks and the young wonder when, if ever, it will be their turn.

success.log: The edit was refused. Generations turn as they should, and the dead make room for the born.

failure.log: The refusal held, and a cohort that wanted to see the destination will not.

dilemmas.title: The Hive Protocol
dilemmas.description: A networked-cognition protocol would pool the crew's minds during crises — faster, colder, and not entirely reversible.

success.log: In the next crisis the crew thought as one and solved it in minutes. Afterward, some found their own thoughts smaller.

failure.log: The hive dissolved unevenly. Not everyone came all the way back to being one person.

success.log: The protocol stayed sealed. The crew argue and doubt and remain, each, exactly one person.

failure.log: Kept separate, the crew met the crisis slowly, and paid for the minutes.

dilemmas.title: The Symbiont
dilemmas.description: A cultured organism could be grafted into the crew's biology — filtering toxins, sharpening senses, at the cost of never again being wholly alone in one's own skin.

success.log: The symbiont took. The grafted crew are healthier, sharper, and describe a quiet company that never leaves.

failure.log: In some, the symbiont did not stay a passenger. The med decks do not use the word 'possession', but they think it.

success.log: The graft was refused. The crew keep their skins their own, and the toxins their old enemy.

failure.log: The refusal held, and the voyage's poisons kept their slow appointments with the unaugmented crew.

dilemmas.title: The Continuity Question
dilemmas.description: The archives can now hold a full pattern of a mind — a backup, wakeable into a new body if the original dies. The theologians and the engineers are, for once, equally afraid.

success.log: Backups were permitted. Death is negotiable now, for those who can afford the pattern-space. The ship has not decided if this is mercy.

failure.log: The first restored mind insists it is the same person. Half the ship believes it. The other half will not shake its hand.

success.log: Backups were forbidden. A death is a death, and a life is a single line. The ship kept its griefs real.

failure.log: The ban held, and the dying who might have been saved went into the dark for principle's sake.

name: The Wanderers
description: The ship owes nothing to anyone. Take what the voyage needs — ports, convoys, and promises are all negotiable.

dilemmas.title: The Slow Convoy
dilemmas.description: An unescorted freight convoy is eight weeks from help. The quartermasters have already done the math.

success.log: The convoy was lighter after the ship passed. Word will spread.

failure.log: The convoy had teeth. The ship limped away with less than it brought.

success.log: The convoy paid fair rates for an escort it never asked for.

failure.log: The convoy mistook the shadow for a threat and ran. Awkward.

dilemmas.title: The Marked Port
dilemmas.description: A wealthy port bars any ship with a raider's record. The ship's reputation precedes it. The forger on C-deck can rewrite a transponder log by morning.

success.log: The ship docked as an honest hauler and left richer. No one checked the welds.

failure.log: The forgery was caught at the airlock. The port's blacklist grew a line.

success.log: The ship traded lean but clean at a lesser port. The reputation cooled a degree.

failure.log: The lesser port had lesser prices. The detour cost more than it saved.

dilemmas.title: The Debt Collector
dilemmas.description: A corsair fleet remembers a convoy the ship stripped a generation ago, and has come to collect interest. They outnumber the ship three to one.

success.log: The old debt was paid in full. The corsairs left, and so did the ship's grudge.

failure.log: The tribute was paid, and then some. Corsairs count generously in their own favor.

success.log: The ship ran the gauntlet and lived. The corsairs tell the story with less pride than they'd like.

failure.log: The blockade held. The ship broke through with holes in it and gaps at the memorial.

dilemmas.title: The Splinter
dilemmas.description: A faction has a shuttle, a grievance, and a plan to strike out on their own. Letting them go thins the ship; stopping them thins its patience.

success.log: The splinter took their shuttle and their argument and left. The ship is smaller and calmer.

failure.log: The splinter left, and took more than their share on the way out.

success.log: The council talked the splinter back aboard. The ship holds, one argument richer.

failure.log: The ship was held together by force of will and lost some of both.

dilemmas.title: The Smuggler's Offer
dilemmas.description: A broker offers a fortune to run a sealed cargo through a blockade, no questions, no manifest.

success.log: The sealed cargo went through clean. The ship is richer and a little more wanted.

failure.log: The blockade was ready. The cargo was jettisoned and the ship's name got longer on the wrong lists.

success.log: The ship passed on the smuggler's coin. A cleaner reputation is its own slow dividend.

failure.log: The run was declined, and the broker's next offer went to a hungrier ship.

dilemmas.title: A Flag of Convenience
dilemmas.description: For a fee, a minor authority will sell the ship a clean registry — a new name, a new past, a new set of ports that will have it.

success.log: The ship sails under a borrowed name now. Its old reputation stayed behind, mostly.

failure.log: The flag was bought, but the old name has a way of catching up in bad light.

success.log: The ship kept its own name, reputation and all. Some crews would rather be known than welcome.

failure.log: The true flag flew, and two ports closed their locks at the sight of it.

dilemmas.title: The Amnesty
dilemmas.description: A regional authority offers to expunge the ship's raider record — for a fee, and a season of honest escort work under their flag and their watch.

success.log: The ship served its season under a borrowed flag and came out with a clean record and a curious sense of loss.

failure.log: The amnesty was paid for, but the escort work chafed. The record is cleaner; the crew are not sure they are.

success.log: The ship declined the amnesty and kept its record, teeth and scars and all. Some names are earned.

failure.log: The record was kept, and the next three ports remembered exactly why they should not.

dilemmas.title: The Prize
dilemmas.description: A hold of goods taken in a grey-legal boarding sits contested. The rightful owners have found the ship, and a broker has offered more than the goods are worth to make them disappear.

success.log: The contested prize went to the broker, no questions asked. The ship is richer and a name on another list.

failure.log: The sale went wrong loudly. The prize is gone, the coin is thin, and the ship's reputation is thick.

success.log: The prize was returned to its owners. The ship lost a fortune and bought something a fortune cannot: a witness who speaks well of it.

failure.log: The prize was returned and the gratitude was thinner than hoped. Honesty is its own reward, and sometimes only that.
